key_insight: High-pressure torsion nanostructures TiZrHfNbTa so strongly that phonon transport is suppressed much more than electron transport, making the alloy’s thermal behavior change differently from its electrical behavior.
engineering_relevance: This suggests severe plastic deformation can be used to tune refractory high-entropy alloys for targeted thermal management while preserving useful electrical conduction.
method: Applied high-pressure torsion (HPT) severe plastic deformation to equiatomic TiZrHfNbTa.
Measured thermal conductivity, electrical conductivity, specific heat, and hardness across strain levels.
Related transport changes to dislocation accumulation, ~40 nm grain refinement, and partial BCC-to-omega transformation.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a monotonic drop in electrical conductivity, a low-strain decrease then high-strain partial recovery in thermal conductivity, and a Wiedemann–Franz analysis showing electrons dominate heat transport while phonons contribute only 11–23%.
watch_item: The property evolution is strain-dependent and non-monotonic, so processing windows must be controlled carefully if the goal is reproducible thermal/electrical performance.
paper_title: Thermal and electrical conductivity of a refractory high-entropy alloy after high-pressure torsion: Electron versus phonon contributions
